Greetings from Los Angeles, where I=E2=80=99m currently evacuating my home =
as we speak, and welcome back to In the Room. My thoughts are with all of m=
y neighbors in Hollywood, and all of you in the Palisades, Malibu, Altadena=
, Pasadena, and elsewhere who have lost homes, businesses, schools, or othe=
rwise been affected by the fires. Meanwhile, a sincere thanks to all of the=
 local reporters and production crews who have been doing a truly excellent=
 job covering these fires. My Puck partner and fellow Angeleno Peter Hamby =
and I
just finished recording a special edition of
The Powers That Be about the local media response, which will post here ( h=
ttps://puck.news/podcasts/the-powers-that-be/?utm_campaign=3DIn+the+Room+-+=
SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F8%2F25%29&utm_content=3DIn+The+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2=
F3%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6=
c60617da42abd12a ) tomorrow morning. Stay safe out there.

lic-press-bezos-soon-shiong-billionaire-owners-endorsements-scandal-los-ang=

essay was a modern exemplar of the righteous alarm-sounding, choir-preachin=
g,
cry-in-a-canyon genre. Its straightforward thesis was really best articulat=
ed in the subhed: =E2=80=9CThe billionaire class has proved itself a poor s=
teward of media. Journalists must redouble their efforts to expose the thre=
at to democracy.=E2=80=9D

=C2=A0

Hours later, Jann Wenner hit reply-all: =E2=80=9CNorm: Terrific summary of =

y=E2=80=99s
decision to settle Trump=E2=80=99s defamation lawsuit against ABC News with=

c-news-after-trump-settlement/?utm_campaign=3DIn+the+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%28=

=C2=A0

That evening, Parenting magazine founder Robin Wolaner told Norm she was =
=E2=80=9Csharing your brilliant column widely.=E2=80=9D Ron Shapiro, the fa=
med sports agent and attorney, commended his freshman roommate (Haverford, =

sday, Joel
Simon, the director of the Craig Newmark School=E2=80=99s Journalism Protec=
tion Initiative, joined the chorus: =E2=80=9CI read this piece as a call fo=
r action and an admonition that we in journalism must come together and fie=
rcely defend our rights,=E2=80=9D he wrote. =E2=80=9CNo one else is going t=

=C2=A0

You can forgive this generation=E2=80=94and, indeed, this industry=E2=80=94=
for its sense of moral outrage and comparative helplessness
during the afterglow of their brilliant careers. Trump=E2=80=99s first term=
 was a golden era for the thriving #Resistance marketplace that Norm and hi=
s circle ostensibly long for=E2=80=94one in which media outlets like CNN, M=
SNBC, The New York Times, and The Washington Post saw record subscriber and=
 audience growth, and Trump journalists (Maggie, Swan, Dawsey, Acosta, et a=
l.) achieved lucrative, if fleeting, pseudo-celebrity

1736358784&utm_campaign=3DIn+the+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F8%2F25%29&utm_co=
ntent=3DIn+The+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F3%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_actio=
n&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) on November 6, t=
he day after the election, that marketplace has been decimated and is unlik=
ely to return with similar force outside of a few obvious muted venues, suc=
h as The Atlantic=E2=80=94itself run by a more sanguine billionaire.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

This phenomenon
mirrors the =E2=80=9Cun-resistance ( https://www.semafor.com/article/01/07/=
2025/were-not-them-democrats-silence-on-jan-6-heralds-a-new-un-resistance?u=
tm_campaign=3DIn+the+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F8%2F25%29&utm_content=3DIn+T=
he+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F3%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=
=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a )=E2=80=9D of the Democratic P=
arty itself. Of course, the Joy Reid genre of =E2=80=9CKamala ran a =E2=80=
=98flawless=E2=80=99 campaign=E2=80=9D liberal punditry has proven to be si=
loed, ineffective, and exhausting. But, more importantly, the aforementione=
d businesses, with the exception of the
Times, have been significantly diminished both by market forces and the ine=
ptitude of their leaders (a familiar refrain here, I know).

=C2=A0

Perhaps most dispiriting for Norm and his ilk, it seems, is the lingering s=
ense that the priorities of the billionaire media owners appear to have cha=
nged so dramatically and, to add insult to injury, so unapologetically. Obv=
iously, Elon
Musk=E2=80=99s X is a very different animal than Jack Dorsey=E2=80=99s Twit=
ter. But many others have given the impression of a stunning volte-face. Ma=
rk Zuckerberg=E2=80=99s decision to eliminate fact-checking from Facebook a=
nd Instagram after investing in it heavily following Trump=E2=80=99s 2016 v=
ictory, and the decision to roll the policy out on Fox News, has been widel=
y portrayed as the latest and most extraordinary capitulation by the billio=
naire class (never mind that
Zuck has been moving toward this for some time). Bezos=E2=80=99s newfound o=
ptimism and even enthusiasm about Trump, which he articulated ( https://www=
=2Enytimes.com/video/business/dealbook/=
100000009860058/jeff-bezos-trump-dealb=
ook.html?smid=3Durl-share&utm_campaign=3DIn+the+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F8=
%2F25%29&utm_content=3DIn+The+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F3%2F25%29&utm_mediu=
m=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) a=
t DealBook, stands in stark contrast to the man who stood side by side with=
 Marty Baron at Jamal Khashoggi=E2=80=99s memorial and helped conceive =E2=
=80=9CDemocracy Dies
in Darkness=E2=80=9D as the Post=E2=80=99s slogan.

Speaking of The Washington Post=E2=80=A6
----------------------------------

Indeed, the current drama at the Post epitomizes these
changing times. Bezos=E2=80=99s decision to block the Harris endorsement, o=
p-ed editor David Shipley=E2=80=99s decision to kill a critical cartoon of =
Bezos and other tech titans abasing themselves ( https://www.npr.org/2025/0=
1/04/nx-s1-5248299/cartoonist-quits-wapo-over-bezos-trump-cartoon-washingto=
npost?utm_campaign=3DIn+the+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F8%2F25%29&utm_content=
=3DIn+The+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F3%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm=
_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) before Trump, and the=
 exodus of top political reporters like Josh Dawsey (to the
Journal), Tyler Pager (to the Times), and Ashley Parker and Michael Scherer=
 (to The Atlantic)=E2=80=94these are all significant data points suggesting=
 that this is not Baron=E2=80=99s paper anymore. Meanwhile, Will Lewis=E2=
=80=99s appointment as publisher and C.E.O. feels like an intentional affro=
nt to the pride and sanctimony of reporters who see journalism as a noble c=
alling, rather than a for-profit
business. In telling a proud newsroom that =E2=80=9Cno one is reading your =
stuff,=E2=80=9D reminding union members that they=E2=80=99re welcome to qui=
t if they don=E2=80=99t like his policies, and publicly calling NPR=E2=80=
=99s media reporter =E2=80=9Can activist,=E2=80=9D Lewis has proved to be a=
 reckless bull among sacred cows.

=C2=A0

But that, of course, is the point. Bezos acknowledged for more than a year =
that financial success, or at least
financial stability, is his top priority for the Post. And while it can be =
hard to support the idea that alienating your core audience and losing your=
 best-known reporters is good for business, it was also impossible to defen=
d an existing strategy that resulted in around $100 million in annual losse=
s, a near-constant state of internal corporate warfare, and cutting hundred=
s of employees every year. (The Post laid off another 4 percent of its staf=
f this week. And given
that this won=E2=80=99t make much of a dent in its deficit, more is sure to=
 come.)=C2=A0

=C2=A0

As for the Dawsey & Co. defections, well, that almost certainly wasn=E2=80=
=99t part of the plan, and certainly emphasizes Lewis=E2=80=99s leadership =
shortcomings and lack of finesse. At the same time, these journalists=E2=80=
=99 =E2=80=9Cstar power=E2=80=9D should probably also be contextualized by =
the fact that, well, no one was reading their stuff.

=C2=A0

In any event, it=E2=80=99s understandable that dispirited liberals would lo=
ok at the current media landscape and fear that the pillar of the fourth es=
tate, itself, was crumbling. But as Semafor=E2=80=99s Liz Hoffman has noted=
, the current rightward lurch of tech and media leaders was hardly any more=
 theatrical than the progressive shift many of these people made nearly a d=
ecade ago. Meanwhile, none of the hard-hitting
journalism that really matters to the future of the Republic is actually th=
reatened by Bezos=E2=80=99s decisions to forgo an obvious and ineffectual p=
residential endorsement, or Shipley=E2=80=99s move to scrap a rather uninsp=
ired cartoon, nor even Iger=E2=80=99s decision to settle a defamation lawsu=
it in order to save his company and network from suffering far greater fina=
ncial and reputational damage. The real, needle-moving, deeply sourced jour=
nalism is still being championed and maintained across all these
institutions, including the Post. In fact, one of the Post=E2=80=99s genuin=
e challenges is that liberal political journalism is actually oversaturated=
, and that the market doesn=E2=80=99t demand 500 political reporters coveri=
ng the same 55,000-square-foot White House.=C2=A0

Follow the
Money
----------

Instead, the more significant threat to the fourth estate is the
decline of these media businesses, themselves, which in turn diminish their=
 clout. As Politico=E2=80=99s John Harris argued a few years back, =E2=80=
=9Cthe dilution of institutional power in an age of media hyper-saturation=
=E2=80=9D has deprived legacy media organizations of their influence, which=
, in turn, =E2=80=9Cis a gift to public officials seeking to evade accounta=
bility.=E2=80=9D And this problem is especially acute at legacy organizatio=
ns that have suffered under bad owners or bad leaders who
accelerated the pace of decline. But the weakening of the fourth estate isn=
=E2=80=99t happening now and because of Trump=E2=80=99s election=E2=80=94ra=
ther, it started years ago, and presaged it.

=C2=A0

In that regard, what Norm and his friends should really be pining for are s=
uccessful business models that will allow journalistic institutions to endu=
re, and to do so independent of the generosity of any
billionaire benefactor. In other words, exactly what Bezos, himself, is pin=
ing for at the Post, which, as he put it more than a year ago, is profitabi=
lity and =E2=80=9Cfinancial success.=E2=80=9D At present, it seems as thoug=
h he and Lewis intend to get there by transforming the company into a nonpa=
rtisan global news report that doesn=E2=80=99t depend on star talent or par=
tisan affinity=E2=80=94The Economist meets USA Today, perhaps, or whatever =
Semafor initially set out to be when it aspired to
reach 200 million college-educated English readers. It=E2=80=99s actually a=
n incredibly noble business goal, and even an editorial one, too.

=C2=A0

Whether Jeff and Will know how to get there is another matter. Bezos said a=
t DealBook that he has some unspecified =E2=80=9Cideas,=E2=80=9D but time m=
ay prove that he was far too busy with other pursuits in global and interga=
lactic domination to be the savior the Post needed, and
that Will was, in fact, the unscrupulous charlatan that his critics have co=
lored him as. Alternatively, they may have a plan that takes years to execu=
te, but which will render the Baron-era romanticism a footnote. But they we=
re never going to get where they wanted to go with the paper Fred Ryan left=
 them, nor is Bezos likely to be satisfied with an only marginally profitab=
le business that caters to an audience of mere millions. The Times may be t=
he benchmark for success
in this industry, but Bezos is used to far higher profit margins.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

In another reply on the email thread, the Pulitzer Prize-winning reporter J=
onathan Kaufman recalled running into Norm years ago in the Bloomberg L.P. =
lobby in New York, where the seasoned journalist told him: =E2=80=9CYou kno=
w what I have learned in my years of journalism, Jonathan? Choose your bill=
ionaires wisely.=E2=80=9D
Like many on the chain, Kaufman had concluded that =E2=80=9Cour current cro=
p of billionaires=E2=80=9D weren=E2=80=99t the answer.=C2=A0

On the other hand, who else would be able to tolerate years of financial lo=
sses, union fights, internal headaches, and shifting political winds while =
the industry figures out what the hell it=E2=80=99s going to do? And what i=
f=E2=80=94just what if=E2=80=94these transformations, however painful in th=
e moment, actually work out? Lost in all the sturm und drang about
the Post is the fact that, without Bezos, it would likely be out of busines=
s.

argin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Indeed, the current drama at the <em>Post </em>=

<em>Journal</em>), <strong>Tyler Pager </strong>(to the <em>Times</em>), an=

E.O. feels like an intentional affront to the pride and sanctimony of repor=
ters who see journalism as a noble calling, rather than a for-profit
business. In telling a proud newsroom that =E2=80=9Cno one is reading your =
stuff,=E2=80=9D reminding union members that they=E2=80=99re welcome to qui=

=99s media reporter =E2=80=9Can activist,=E2=80=9D Lewis has proved to be a=
 reckless bull among sacred cows.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px=
;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">But that, of course, =
is the point. Bezos acknowledged for more than <em>a year </em>that <em>fin=
ancial success</em>, or at least
<em>financial stability</em>, is his top priority for the <em>Post</em>. An=
d while it can be hard to support the idea that alienating your core audien=
ce and losing your best-known reporters is good for business, it was also i=
mpossible to defend an existing strategy that resulted in around $100 milli=
on in annual losses, a near-constant state of internal corporate warfare, a=
nd cutting hundreds of employees every year. (The <em>Post </em>laid off an=
other 4 percent of its staff this week. And given
that this won=E2=80=99t make much of a dent in its deficit, more is sure to=
 come.)=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p styl=
e=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">As for the Dawsey & Co. defections, w=
ell, that almost certainly wasn=E2=80=99t part of <em>the plan</em>, and ce=
rtainly emphasizes Lewis=E2=80=99s leadership shortcomings and lack of fine=
sse. At the same time, these journalists=E2=80=99 =E2=80=9Cstar power=E2=80=
=9D should probably also be contextualized by the fact that, well, no one w=
as reading their stuff.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0<=
/p><p style=3D"margin:0;">In any event, it=E2=80=99s understandable that di=
spirited liberals would look at the current media landscape and fear that t=
he pillar of the fourth estate, itself, was crumbling. But as Semafor=E2=80=
=99s <strong>Liz Hoffman</strong> has noted, the current rightward lurch of=
 tech and media leaders was hardly any more theatrical than the progressive=
 shift many of these people made nearly a decade ago. Meanwhile, <em>none <=
/em>of the hard-hitting
journalism that really matters to the future of the Republic is actually th=
reatened by Bezos=E2=80=99s decisions to forgo an obvious and ineffectual p=
residential endorsement, or Shipley=E2=80=99s move to scrap a rather uninsp=
ired cartoon, nor even Iger=E2=80=99s decision to settle a defamation lawsu=
it in order to save his company and network from suffering far greater fina=
ncial and reputational damage. The real, needle-moving, deeply sourced jour=
nalism is still being championed and maintained across all these
institutions, including the <em>Post</em>. In fact, one of the <em>Post</em=
>=E2=80=99s genuine challenges is that liberal political journalism is actu=
ally <em>oversaturated</em>, and that the market doesn=E2=80=99t demand 500=
 political reporters covering the same 55,000-square-foot White House.=C2=

<table class=3D"c4" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspaci=
ng=3D"0" role=3D"presentation" style=3D"mso-table-lspace:0pt;mso-table-rspa=
ce:0pt;word-break:break-word;"><tbody><tr><td class=3D"r" style=3D"padding-=
bottom:32px;"><div style=3D"color:#000000;direction:ltr;font-family:'In=
ter','Arial';font-size:17px;font-weight:400;letter-spacing:0px;=
line-height:150%;text-align:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=3D"m=
argin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Instead, the more significant threat to the fou=
rth estate is the
decline of these media businesses, themselves, which in turn diminish their=
 clout. As Politico=E2=80=99s <strong>John Harris </strong>argued a few yea=
rs back, =E2=80=9Cthe dilution of institutional power in an age of media hy=
per-saturation=E2=80=9D has deprived legacy media organizations of their in=
fluence, which, in turn, =E2=80=9Cis a gift to public officials seeking to =
evade accountability.=E2=80=9D And this problem is especially acute at lega=
cy organizations that have suffered under bad owners or bad leaders who
accelerated the pace of decline. But the weakening of the fourth estate isn=
=E2=80=99t happening <em>now</em> and <em>because</em> of Trump=E2=80=99s e=
lection=E2=80=94rather, it started years ago, and presaged it.</p><p style=
=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bott=
om:0px;">In that regard, what Norm and his friends should <em>really</em> b=
e pining for are successful business models that will allow journalistic in=
stitutions to endure, and to do so independent of the generosity of any
billionaire benefactor. In other words, exactly what Bezos, himself, is pin=
ing for at the <em>Post</em>, which, as he put it more than a year ago, is =
profitability and =E2=80=9Cfinancial success.=E2=80=9D At present, it seems=
 as though he and Lewis intend to get there by transforming the company int=
o a nonpartisan global news report that doesn=E2=80=99t depend on star tale=
nt or partisan affinity=E2=80=94<em>The Economist</em> meets <em>USA Today<=
/em>, perhaps, or whatever Semafor initially set out to be when it aspired =
to
reach 200 million college-educated English readers. It=E2=80=99s actually a=
n incredibly noble business goal, and even an editorial one, too.</p><p sty=
le=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bo=
ttom:0px;">Whether Jeff and Will know how to get there is another matter. B=
ezos said at DealBook that he has some unspecified =E2=80=9Cideas,=E2=80=9D=
 but time may prove that he was far too busy with other pursuits in global =
and intergalactic domination to be the savior the <em>Post </em>needed, and
that Will was, in fact, the unscrupulous charlatan that his critics have co=
lored him as. Alternatively, they may have a plan that takes years to execu=
te, but which will render the Baron-era romanticism a footnote. But they we=
re never going to get where they wanted to go with the paper <strong>Fred R=
yan </strong>left them, nor is Bezos likely to be satisfied with an only ma=
rginally profitable business that caters to an audience of mere millions. T=
he <em>Times </em>may be the benchmark for success
in <em>this </em>industry, but Bezos is used to far higher profit margins.=
=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"ma=
rgin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">In another reply on the email thread, the Pulitz=
er Prize-winning reporter <strong>Jonathan Kaufman</strong> recalled runnin=
g into Norm years ago in the Bloomberg L.P. lobby in New York, where the se=
asoned journalist told him: =E2=80=9CYou know what I have learned in my yea=
rs of journalism, Jonathan? Choose your billionaires wisely.=E2=80=9D
Like many on the chain, Kaufman had concluded that =E2=80=9Cour current cro=
p of billionaires=E2=80=9D weren=E2=80=99t the answer.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D=
"margin:0;"><br/>On the other hand, who else would be able to tolerate year=
s of financial losses, union fights, internal headaches, and shifting polit=
ical winds while the industry figures out what the hell it=E2=80=99s going =
to do? And what if=E2=80=94just what <em>if=E2=80=94</em>these transformati=
ons, however painful in the moment, actually work out? Lost in all the <em>=
sturm und drang </em>about
the <em>Post </em>is the fact that, without Bezos, it would likely be out o=
f business.</p></div>
